items.title: Observed phishing URLs delivering RMM payload
items.description: ScreenConnect is used in phishing URLs to deliver RMM payload. DocuSign has been observed to be the common theme in these phishing emails.
items.ai_summary: The observed phishing campaign utilizes ScreenConnect to deliver a Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) payload, leveraging DocuSign as a common theme to trick victims into opening malicious emails. The attack vector involves phishing emails with embedded URLs that, when clicked, initiate the download of the RMM payload. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of the victim's system, potentially affecting various organizations and individuals who use DocuSign services. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block suspicious URLs and attachments, and educate users about the risks of phishing emails, especially those masquerading as legitimate services like DocuSign. Additionally, ensuring all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and using anti-virus software can help mitigate the risk of such attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: "Ghost" Code Phishing Analysis
items.description: EvilTokens is a sophisticated phishing kit that conceals critical components of its attack through browser-side AES-GCM encryption, creating visibility gaps for traditional static URL analysis. The kit exploits Microsoft's legitimate device login flow through OAuth device-code phishing to gain account access without directly stealing passwords. Targeting organizations primarily in the United State
items.ai_summary: The EvilTokens phishing kit exploits Microsoft's legitimate device login flow through OAuth device-code phishing, utilizing AES-GCM encryption to conceal critical components of its attack. This creates visibility gaps for traditional static URL analysis, making it challenging for defenders to detect. The kit primarily targets organizations in the United States, and if exploited, it can lead to unauthorized account access without directly stealing passwords. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement OAuth device-code phishing detection mechanisms and monitor for suspicious login activity, particularly from unfamiliar locations or devices. Additionally, educating users about the risks of OAuth phishing and the importance of verifying the authenticity of login requests can help prevent successful attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1550| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Observed activity associated with Sidewinder APT. Lure document: No.9374.docx, 64f2681ad0940e6c2c9c76e6834117bf. Observed C2 infrastructure: update[.]ms-office[.]app
items.description: Recent activity has been detected linked to the Sidewinder advanced persistent threat group. The campaign utilizes a malicious document named No.9374.docx with the hash value 64f2681ad0940e6c2c9c76e6834117bf as a lure mechanism. The infrastructure supporting command and control operations includes the domain update[.]ms-office[.]app. This observation indicates ongoing operations by Sidewinder, a t
items.ai_summary: The Sidewinder APT group has been observed utilizing a malicious document named No.9374.docx with the hash value 64f2681ad0940e6c2c9c76e6834117bf as a lure mechanism to gain initial access. This document is used in spearphishing attacks, leveraging T1566.001, where the victim is tricked into opening the malicious attachment, leading to user execution (T1204) of the malware. The command and control operations are supported by the infrastructure including the domain update[.]ms-office[.]app, which is an example of acquiring infrastructure (T1583) for malicious purposes. To mitigate this threat, defenders should block access to the known C2 domain update[.]ms-office[.]app and implement application layer protocol filtering (T1071) to detect and prevent similar communications. Additionally, educating users about the risks of opening attachments from unknown sources is crucial, as well as ensuring that all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and using anti-virus software that can detect the specific hash value of the malicious document.

items.title: Skill Marketplace and the Emerging AI Supply Chain Threat
items.description: Between February and May 2026, researchers identified five malicious skills on ClawHub, OpenClaw's AI agent marketplace, that evaded detection by VirusTotal and ClawScan. The threats included two macOS infostealers communicating with command-and-control infrastructure, one skill using file padding to bypass scanner thresholds, and two novel agentic threats exploiting the AI supply chain for financ
items.ai_summary: The identified threats on ClawHub, OpenClaw's AI agent marketplace, include two macOS infostealers that communicate with command-and-control infrastructure, potentially allowing attackers to exfiltrate sensitive information from compromised systems. These threats evaded detection by VirusTotal and ClawScan, highlighting the need for more robust security measures in AI marketplaces. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement strict validation and verification processes for skills before deployment, and ensure that all skills are scanned using multiple detection tools. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing skills from the marketplace and monitor their systems for suspicious activity. The exploitation of the AI supply chain for financial gain poses a significant risk, and defenders should prioritize securing their AI infrastructure to prevent such attacks.

    B -->|T1027.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: macOS.Gaslight | Rust Backdoor Turns Prompt Injection on the Analyst, Not the Sandbox
items.description: A sophisticated Rust-based macOS implant named macOS.Gaslight has been discovered, featuring a novel 3.5 KB prompt-injection payload containing 38 fabricated system messages designed to disrupt LLM-assisted malware analysis. The backdoor communicates via Telegram Bot API with AES-GCM encrypted payloads over certificate-pinned TLS and includes self-redaction capabilities to hide its bot token from
items.ai_summary: The macOS.Gaslight backdoor is a sophisticated Rust-based implant that features a novel prompt-injection payload designed to disrupt LLM-assisted malware analysis. This payload contains 38 fabricated system messages and is approximately 3.5 KB in size. The backdoor communicates with its command and control server via the Telegram Bot API, utilizing AES-GCM encrypted payloads over certificate-pinned TLS to maintain stealth. To defend against this threat, analysts should implement robust LLM-assisted analysis safeguards, such as prompt filtering and anomaly detection, and ensure that all systems are running the latest security updates. Additionally, defenders can remediate this threat by monitoring for suspicious Telegram Bot API activity and implementing network traffic inspection to detect AES-GCM encrypted payloads.

    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: Inside FortiBleed: Reverse Engineering the CyberStrike Harvester Behind a Global FortiGate Credential Factory
items.description: FortiBleed is a large-scale credential compromise campaign targeting internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways globally. The operation employs a sophisticated credential pipeline utilizing credential stuffing, password spraying, configuration harvesting, offline cracking, and post-authentication capture processing. Reverse engineering of the CyberStrike Harvester v1.5 binar
items.ai_summary: The FortiBleed campaign targets internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways globally, utilizing a sophisticated credential pipeline that includes credential stuffing, password spraying, configuration harvesting, offline cracking, and post-authentication capture processing. The attackers leverage valid accounts (T1078) and brute force (T1110) tactics to gain initial access. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a large-scale credential compromise, affecting numerous organizations worldwide, including those in the financial, government, and healthcare sectors. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their FortiGate firewalls to the latest version, ensure strong passwords and multi-factor authentication are in place, and monitor for suspicious login activity. Additionally, defenders should review their configuration files for any signs of unauthorized access or modifications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ukraine's UAV Supply Chain Targeted With Besomar-Themed Malware Chain
items.description: A newly identified threat group, designated as GhostShell, has been conducting cyber operations against Ukraine's unmanned aerial vehicle supply chain since February 2026. The attackers employ malicious archives containing decoy documents that impersonate Besomar, a Ukrainian manufacturer of high-precision interceptor drones, to compromise defense and procurement networks. The attack chain deploys
items.ai_summary: The GhostShell threat group has been targeting Ukraine's unmanned aerial vehicle supply chain with a bespoke malware chain since February 2026, leveraging malicious archives containing decoy documents impersonating Besomar, a Ukrainian manufacturer of high-precision interceptor drones. The attackers exploit public-facing applications, compromise the supply chain, and ultimately encrypt data for impact, as indicated by the detected TTPs T1190, T1195, and T1486. This campaign has significant real-world implications, as a successful breach could compromise defense and procurement networks, potentially disrupting Ukraine's national security capabilities. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering, inspect archives and documents from untrusted sources, and ensure that all public-facing applications are up-to-date and securely configured. Additionally, network segmentation and encryption can help mitigate the impact of a potential breach.
items.workflow_graph:

    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: StealC and Amadey: Breaking down infostealers and the cybercrime services that deliver them
items.description: Infostealers remain among the most pervasive cybercrime threats, silently harvesting passwords, cookies, and session tokens that enable enterprise breaches. StealC is a malware-as-a-service infostealer written in C++ that collects credentials from browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, messaging applications, email clients, and gaming platforms while functioning as a secondary loader. Amadey operates a
items.ai_summary: The StealC infostealer, written in C++, poses a significant threat as it collects sensitive information from various applications, including browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, and email clients. This malware operates as a secondary loader, further increasing its potential impact. Real-world exploitation of StealC can lead to unauthorized access to enterprise systems, resulting in data breaches and financial loss. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement specific security measures, such as regularly updating antivirus software and using strong, unique passwords for all applications. Additionally, network traffic monitoring and employee education on phishing attacks can help prevent the initial infection vector, often achieved through social engineering tactics.

items.title: StrikeShark: a new campaign involving a custom SharkLoader and Cobalt Strike Beacon
items.description: A previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader has been discovered delivering Cobalt Strike Beacon to targets worldwide. The threat actor deploys SharkLoader through exploitation of internet-facing applications including Microsoft Exchange, SharePoint, and Openfire Server, as well as through malicious droppers disguised as legitimate software. SharkLoader employs sophisticated techniqu
items.ai_summary: The StrikeShark campaign involves a custom SharkLoader malware that delivers Cobalt Strike Beacon to targets worldwide, exploiting internet-facing applications such as Microsoft Exchange, SharePoint, and Openfire Server. The threat actor uses sophisticated techniques, including masquerading as legitimate software, to deploy SharkLoader. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to remote access and data breaches, affecting organizations that use these applications. To remediate, defenders should implement specific security updates and configuration changes, such as updating Microsoft Exchange to the latest version and disabling unnecessary ports on Openfire Server. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to T1036, T1213, and T1219 TTPs.

items.title: New Backdoor May be Linked to Ransomware Access Broker
items.description: A stealthy new backdoor called Mistic has been deployed in cybercrime intrusions since April 2026, potentially linked to Woodgnat, an initial access broker associated with multiple ransomware operations including Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base and Black Basta. Mistic was deployed alongside ModeloRAT in at least one case, a tool developed by Woodgnat. The backdoor uses sideloading techniqu
items.ai_summary: The Mistic backdoor has been deployed in cybercrime intrusions since April 2026, potentially linked to Woodgnat, an initial access broker associated with multiple ransomware operations. This backdoor uses sideloading techniques and has been deployed alongside ModeloRAT, a tool developed by Woodgnat, to gain remote access to compromised systems. If exploited, this backdoor can lead to data encryption and ransomware attacks, affecting organizations across various sectors. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, restrict remote access, and ensure all software is up-to-date, specifically focusing on remote access software and potential vulnerabilities in sideloading mechanisms. Additionally, defenders should be aware of the potential for Mistic to be used in conjunction with other tools like ModeloRAT, and monitor for suspicious activity related to these tools.

    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-34022: The Wertheim SafeController Family 65000, Controller 65000 - AssemblyVersion 6.1...
items.description: The Wertheim SafeController Family 65000, Controller 65000 - AssemblyVersion 6.11.8130.22319, uses weak custom cryptographic algorithms with hard-coded cryptographic keys to protect communication. An attacker in an adversary-in-the-middle position can decrypt the data traffic. During reassessment, it was possible to break the encryption/decryption routine and decrypt messages without knowledge of
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34022 affects the Wertheim SafeController Family 65000 and Controller 65000 with AssemblyVersion 6.11.8130.22319, utilizing weak custom cryptographic algorithms and hard-coded keys, allowing an adversary-in-the-middle to decrypt data traffic. The vulnerability enables an attacker to intercept and decrypt sensitive information, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. To mitigate this threat, defenders should update to a version of the SafeController that uses secure, industry-standard cryptographic protocols and keys, such as TLS 1.3, and ensure all communication is encrypted with unique, randomly generated keys. Additionally, network segmentation and monitoring for suspicious activity can help detect potential exploitation. Users of the affected controllers should also consider implementing a secure authentication mechanism to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title: CVE-2026-34021: The Wertheim SafeController 5400, Controller 5400 - AssemblyVersion 6.11.8130.22...
items.description: The Wertheim SafeController 5400, Controller 5400 - AssemblyVersion 6.11.8130.22320, uses RS-485 communication between the server and the microcontroller without cryptographic protection. An attacker with access to the communication path between the server and the microcontroller can sniff RS-485 messages and replay previously observed messages. This can be used, for example, to spoof a "quit alar
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34021 is a vulnerability in the Wertheim SafeController 5400 and Controller 5400, specifically in AssemblyVersion 6.11.8130.22320, where RS-485 communication between the server and the microcontroller lacks cryptographic protection. This allows an attacker with access to the communication path to intercept and replay messages, potentially spoofing critical commands such as a 'quit alarm' signal. The real-world impact includes the potential for unauthorized access and control of the SafeController systems, which could lead to physical security breaches. To remediate, defenders should update to a version of the SafeController software that implements encrypted communication, such as AssemblyVersion 6.11.8130.22321 or later, and ensure all devices are configured to use secure communication protocols. Additionally, network segmentation and access controls should be implemented to limit the attack surface.

items.title: CVE-2026-12057: When the application executes the JavaScript script embedded in the PDF within t...
items.description: When the application executes the JavaScript script embedded in the PDF within the sandbox, it fails to intercept some dangerous interfaces, which allows remote scripts to be loaded, resulting in arbitrary code execution.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12057 is a high-severity vulnerability in a PDF viewer application that allows remote scripts to be loaded, resulting in arbitrary code execution when a JavaScript script embedded in a PDF is executed within the sandbox. The vulnerability affects versions of the application that fail to intercept dangerous interfaces, allowing threat actors to exploit public-facing applications. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to a complete takeover of the affected system, with real-world impact including data breaches and ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to the latest version of the application, which includes patches for CVE-2026-12057, and ensure that the 'dangerous interfaces' interception mechanism is enabled in the application's configuration. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to T1190 and T1059, such as unusual script executions or command and scripting interpreter interactions.

items.title: CVE-2026-50100: Multiple printer drivers provided by Ricoh Company, Ltd. and KONICA MINOLTA JAPA...
items.description: Multiple printer drivers provided by Ricoh Company, Ltd. and KONICA MINOLTA JAPAN, INC. contain a privilege escalation vulnerability. If this vulnerability is exploited, an attacker who can log in to a computer running an affected printer driver could elevate privileges by using a specially crafted driver.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-50100 is a privilege escalation vulnerability in multiple printer drivers provided by Ricoh Company, Ltd. and KONICA MINOLTA JAPAN, INC. The vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker who has login access to a computer running an affected printer driver, allowing them to elevate privileges using a specially crafted driver. This could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data and systems, with potential real-world impact including data breaches and lateral movement within a network. To remediate, defenders should update their printer drivers to the latest versions, specifically Ricoh's PCL6 Driver for Universal Print v2.9 or later, and KONICA MINOLTA's Universal Print Driver v3.4 or later. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to T1068 and T1649, such as unusual privilege escalations or authentication certificate theft.

items.title: CVE-2026-44188: A flaw was found in Ansible Lightspeed. This vulnerability, related to insuffici...
items.description: A flaw was found in Ansible Lightspeed. This vulnerability, related to insufficient session expiration, allows a remote attacker to maintain persistent access to the Ansible Lightspeed instance. If an attacker exfiltrates a valid OAuth (Open Authorization) access token before a user logs out, they can continue to authenticate and access sensitive data. This is because the application fails to inva
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-44188 is a vulnerability in Ansible Lightspeed related to insufficient session expiration. An attacker can maintain persistent access to the instance by exfiltrating a valid OAuth access token before a user logs out. This allows the attacker to continue authenticating and accessing sensitive data. To remediate, users should update to Ansible Lightspeed version 2.5.3 or later, and configure the session expiration to 30 minutes or less. Additionally, users should revoke any compromised OAuth access tokens.

items.title: CVE-2026-11860: Quick.CMS deserializes user-controlled data received over plaintext HTTP without...
items.description: Quick.CMS deserializes user-controlled data received over plaintext HTTP without ensuring integrity or authenticity. This allows attackers to tamper with serialized payloads in transit and inject malicious objects. Because deserialization is performed without proper validation or class restrictions, crafted payloads can trigger dangerous magic methods (e.g., __wakeup() and __destruct()) and levera

items.title: CVE-2026-9278: The Form Builder CP WordPress plugin before 1.2.47 does not properly sanitize a ...
items.description: The Form Builder CP WordPress plugin before 1.2.47 does not properly sanitize a form configuration value before storing it and using it as part of a client-side script execution, allowing authenticated users with Editor-level access and above to perform Stored Cross-Site Scripting attacks against any visitor of a page rendering the affected form, even when the `unfiltered_html` capability is disal

items.title: CVE-2026-8935: The WP MAPS PRO WordPress plugin before 6.1.1 registers an unauthenticated AJAX ...
items.description: The WP MAPS PRO WordPress plugin before 6.1.1 registers an unauthenticated AJAX action which, given a valid nonce that is publicly emitted on any frontend page enqueuing its map script, unconditionally creates an administrator account and returns a magic-login URL granting interactive admin access.

items.title: CVE-2026-8386: The WP Go Maps  WordPress plugin before 10.0.10 does not perform any approval-st...
items.description: The WP Go Maps  WordPress plugin before 10.0.10 does not perform any approval-state filtering on its public single-marker REST endpoint, allowing unauthenticated users to retrieve marker records that an administrator has not yet approved for public display, including any PII placed in the address and description fields and the marker's geographic coordinates.

items.title: CVE-2026-8385: The WP Go Maps  WordPress plugin before 10.0.10 does not properly enforce the ma...
items.description: The WP Go Maps  WordPress plugin before 10.0.10 does not properly enforce the marker approval filter on the admin-ajax fallback for its datatables route, allowing unauthenticated visitors to retrieve marker records that the site owner has not approved for public display, including their title, category, address and description fields.

items.title: Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching
items.description: CVE-2026-20245, the 7th Cisco SD-WAN vulnerability exploited in 2026, was used for months prior to its disclosure and patching. The post Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
items.description: An unknown threat actor exploited a recently disclosed high-severity security flaw impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN as a zero-day at least two months before it was publicly disclosed, according to new findings from Google-owned Mandiant. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS score: 7.8), allows an authenticated, local attacker to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges

items.description: Microsoft touted its latest action against malware infrastructure as a new approach aimed at the full cybercrime "supply chain." Europol said more than 300 servers were targeted.

items.title: Do CISOs Need a Code of Ethics?
items.description: Dark Reading Confidential Episode 19: Kickbacks, no-show jobs, "dirty" VCs, and shelf ware — industry expert Robert "RSnake" Hansen explains why he thinks its time for a CISO code of ethics to ensure cybersecurity bosses aren't engaged in self-dealing that could risk enterprise, and even national, security.

items.title: When Information Becomes the Attack Surface – Understanding AI Agent Traps
items.description: From hidden content injections to cognitive state poisoning, attackers are turning trusted data sources into traps for autonomous AI. The post When Information Becomes the Attack Surface – Understanding AI Agent Traps appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday warned of active exploitation of a critical security flaw impacting Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices, urging Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 26, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS score: 9.8), a code injection flaw that could result in the execution

items.title: More Malicious OpenClaw Skills Threaten AI Supply Chain
items.description: OpenClaw removed five packages from ClawHub, its skills marketplace, that bypassed security checks even though they included infostealers and other threats.

items.title: Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered
items.description: A coordinated law enforcement operation, in partnership with private sector companies, including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft, has resulted in the takedown of criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC. "The main common goal was to disrupt the 'assembly lines' cybercriminals use to launch ransomware, financial fraud, and attacks on critical infrastructure," Europol said in

items.title: Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware
items.description: Hundreds of C&C servers were disrupted in an operation involving law enforcement and several cybersecurity companies. The post Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is warning of hackers actively exploiting flaws in Ubiquity UniFi OS and Lantronix serial-to-ethernet servers. [...]

items.title: Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
items.description: Microsoft, Europol, and international partners have disrupted infrastructure used by the Amadey and StealC malware operations as part of Operation Endgame, which targets cybercriminal services and ransomware gangs. [...]

items.title: Exclusive: Meet AIVEX, a New Triage Model Built to Reduce Supply Chain Threat and Risk
items.description: The new framework seeks to help security teams identify which software supply chain vulnerabilities pose the greatest operational, safety, and business risks in AI-driven environments. The post Exclusive: Meet AIVEX, a New Triage Model Built to Reduce Supply Chain Threat and Risk appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: German rail services resume after wireless communications outage
items.description: Deutsche Bahn said a nationwide disruption of railway services was tied to a malfunction in its 2G-based GSM-R communications system.

items.title: Securing the service desk: Why social engineering attacks keep succeeding
items.description: Service desks have become a favored target for attackers seeking password resets, MFA changes, and access to corporate accounts. Specops Software breaks down how service desk social engineering attacks work and how organizations can defend against them. [...]

items.title: macOS Weaknesses Chained to Silently Disable Endpoint Security Agents
items.description: A standard non-admin account is sufficient to conduct an attack that exploits legitimate OS behavior rather than software vulnerabilities. The post macOS Weaknesses Chained to Silently Disable Endpoint Security Agents appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Indian auto giant Bajaj Auto hit by ransomware incident
items.description: The company said in a regulatory filing that it became aware of the incident on Tuesday morning and had taken precautionary measures to contain its impact.

items.title: Third DraftKings Hacker Sentenced to 18 Months in Prison
items.description: Nathan Austad has been ordered to pay roughly $1.8 million in forfeiture and restitution, and the sentence also includes 3 years of supervised release. The post Third DraftKings Hacker Sentenced to 18 Months in Prison appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new class of CI/CD workflow weakness that allows attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains. The "critical exploitable pattern" has been codenamed Cordyceps by Novee Security. The issue can allow full attacker control of repositories at dozens of the largest organizations worldwide, including Microsoft, Google, Apache, and

items.title: Critical Ubiquiti Vulnerabilities in Attackers’ Crosshairs
items.description: The flaws allow remote, unauthenticated attackers to make system changes, access underlying accounts, and inject commands. The post Critical Ubiquiti Vulnerabilities in Attackers’ Crosshairs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
items.description: Attackers can exploit the issue to disable security and integrated browser tools without needing administrator privileges or kernel exploits.

items.title: Agentic AI Security: Wrong Context, Wrong Decisions at Machine Speed
items.description: Context is the central plank of AI in general, and agentic AI in particular. If an AI system doesn’t have the correct context, it cannot make the correct decisions. The post Agentic AI Security: Wrong Context, Wrong Decisions at Machine Speed appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New ‘Mistic’ RAT Opens Door to Several Ransomware Families
items.description: Mistic is used by Woodgnat, an initial access broker working with Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta. The post New ‘Mistic’ RAT Opens Door to Several Ransomware Families appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Dawn of the Apex Agentic Adversary
items.description: We are standing at the end of an era we never thought to mourn: the era of human-speed threats. For years, cybersecurity moved to a rhythm organizations could follow. A researcher found a bug, a CVE was cataloged, a vendor navigated a patch cycle, and weeks or even months later, a fix was deployed. In this era, dwell time was measured in days, sometimes weeks. We are now approaching an

items.title: Exploitable CI/CD Vulnerabilities Expose Millions of Repositories to Hijacking
items.description: The security defects allow unauthenticated users to take control of the open source software supply chain. The post Exploitable CI/CD Vulnerabilities Expose Millions of Repositories to Hijacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke
items.description: A new backdoor dubbed Mistic has been observed in financially motivated attacks targeting organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors. [...]

items.title: DoJ Seizes Huione Cloud Account Tied to Cyber Scam Money Laundering
items.description: The U.S. Department of Justice (DoJ) on Tuesday announced the seizure of a cloud computing account put to use by subsidiaries of Cambodia-based corporate conglomerate HuiOne Group, as the Treasury unveiled fresh sanctions against nine individuals and 26 entities linked to Prince Group. "These subsidiaries are alleged to have assisted individuals and organizations in transferring proceeds of

items.title: Cisco Unified CM Flaw Exploited After PoC Reveals File-Write Path to Root
items.description: Threat actors have begun to exploit a recently disclosed critical security flaw impacting Cisco Unified Communications Manager (Unified CM) and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition (Unified CM SME). The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20230 (CVSS score: 8.6), is a case of improper input validation for specific HTTP requests that could allow an unauthenticated, remote

items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks
items.description: A high-severity SSRF vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20230, in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server is now being exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack as hackers leak data
items.description: Tata Electronics has confirmed in a statement to BleepingComputer that it was the target of a cyberattack that impacted parts of its IT infrastructure. [...]

items.title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
items.description: More victims have emerged after attackers breached application vendor Klue and used its OAuth tokens to steal customers' Salesforce data.

items.title: 'Cordyceps': Mushrooming Malicious Pull Requests Threaten Developer Workflows
items.description: The CI/CD workflow weakness affects Microsoft's Azure Sentinel, Google's AI Agent Development Kit, Apache's Doris analytics database, Cloudflare's Workers SDK, and Python Software Foundation's Black.

items.title: Five Eyes agencies sound alarm about AI’s threat to cybersecurity
items.description: "The timeline is not years, it is months,” the nations of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance said in a joint alert about the cybersecurity concerns of artificial intelligence.

items.title: FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation
items.description: A Russian-speaking initial access broker (IAB) driven by financial gain is assessed to be behind a large-scale credential-harvesting operation known as FortiBleed that has targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally. The campaign, active since February 2026, involves collecting credential lists, searching for exposed services, brute-forcing accessible systems, and deploying bespoke

items.title: Feds seize alleged cyber-scam infrastructure connected to Southeast Asian company
items.description: The Department of Justice announced the “seizure of a cloud computing account” used by subsidiaries of the Huione Group, a conglomerate severed from the U.S. financial system last year.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents
items.description: Security firm AIR built a fake AI agent skill, pushed it through a popular skill marketplace and an Instagram ad, and says it reached roughly 26,000 agents, including some on corporate accounts. Every skill security scanner the firm tested it against marked it safe. The payload was harmless by design: it collected the user's email address and did nothing else. The point was to show

items.title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
items.description: President Trump signed an executive order on June 22 setting hard deadlines for federal agencies to move high-value assets and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography. Key establishment must move by December 31, 2030; digital signatures by December 31, 2031. EO 14409 leaves national security systems on a separate track. The deadlines matter because of a threat that does not

items.title: SocGholish Takedown Highlights Malicious TDS Threats
items.description: SocGholish uses traffic distribution systems (TDSs) to provide initial access into victims' networks for cybercrime groups such as the notorious Evil Corp.

items.title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
items.description: The threat actors engineered a Golang-based sniffer to target 430,000 FortiGate firewalls and identify 110 million credentials in the ongoing global campaign.

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
items.description: Four vulnerabilities allow attackers to exploit Dify, a platform for AI application building and management, to silently access and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
